content:
# Mark Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), Quire 77, folios 31v–32r (PDF pages 62–63) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_001_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_001_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ This is where the good news of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, begins.

² The prophet Isaiah had written:

"Look—I am sending my messenger ahead of you,
to prepare the road before you.

³ A voice shouts in the wilderness:
'Clear the way for the Lord!
Straighten the path for him!'"

⁴ And so John appeared in the wilderness, baptizing and calling people to be baptized as a sign that they had turned from their sins to receive God's forgiveness.

⁵ People from all across Judea and all of Jerusalem streamed out to him. They were baptized by him in the Jordan River as they openly confessed their sins.

⁶ John wore clothing made of camel's hair with a leather belt at his waist, and he lived on locusts and wild honey.

⁷ He announced to the crowds: "Someone far more powerful than I is coming after me. I am not even worthy to kneel down and untie his sandals.

⁸ I have baptized you in water, but he will baptize you in the Holy Spirit."

⁹ In those days, Jesus came from Nazareth in Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan.

¹⁰ The moment he came up out of the water, he saw the sky torn wide open and the Spirit coming down on him like a dove.

¹¹ And a voice spoke from heaven: "You are my Son, whom I dearly love. You bring me great joy."

¹² At once the Spirit drove him out into the wilderness.

¹³ He stayed in the wilderness for forty days, tested by Satan. He was out among the wild animals, and angels took care of him.

¹⁴ After John was arrested, Jesus went to Galilee and began announcing God's good news:

¹⁵ "The time has come at last," he said. "God's kingdom is here. Turn your lives around and believe this good news!"

¹⁶ One day, walking along the shore of the Sea of Galilee, he spotted Simon and his brother Andrew throwing a net into the water—they were fishermen.

¹⁷ Jesus called out to them, "Come, follow me! I will teach you to fish for people."

¹⁸ They dropped their nets on the spot and followed him.

¹⁹ A little farther along, he saw James and John, the sons of Zebedee, sitting in their boat repairing their nets.

²⁰ He called them immediately, and they left their father Zebedee behind in the boat with the hired crew and followed him.

²¹ They went to Capernaum, and when the Sabbath came, Jesus went straight to the synagogue and began teaching.

²² The people were stunned by his teaching, because he taught with real authority—not like the scribes.

²³ Just then, a man in the grip of an evil spirit cried out from the congregation:

²⁴ "What do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know exactly who you are—the Holy One of God!"

²⁵ Jesus cut him short: "Be quiet! Come out of him!"

²⁶ The evil spirit threw the man into convulsions, let out a piercing shriek, and came out of him.

²⁷ Everyone was shaken. They turned to one another and asked, "What is this? Some kind of new teaching—and it comes with power! He gives orders to evil spirits and they actually obey him!"

²⁸ News about Jesus spread like wildfire across the whole region of Galilee.

²⁹ As soon as they left the synagogue, they went straight to the home of Simon and Andrew, with James and John.

³⁰ Simon's mother-in-law was in bed, burning with fever. They told Jesus about her right away.

³¹ He went to her, took her hand, and helped her up. The fever broke instantly, and she got up and began serving them a meal.

³² That evening, after the sun went down, people brought everyone who was sick or tormented by demons to Jesus.

³³ It seemed like the entire city had crowded around the door.

³⁴ He healed large numbers of people suffering from all kinds of diseases and drove out many demons. He would not let the demons speak, because they knew who he was.

³⁵ Long before dawn, while it was still dark, Jesus got up, slipped away to a deserted place, and prayed.

³⁶ Simon and the others went searching for him.

³⁷ When they found him, they said, "Everyone is looking for you!"

³⁸ But he told them, "Let's move on to the nearby towns—I need to bring the message there too. That's the whole reason I've come."

³⁹ So he traveled all through Galilee, teaching in their synagogues and driving out demons.

⁴⁰ A man with leprosy came and fell on his knees before Jesus, begging him: "If you are willing, you can make me clean."

⁴¹ Deeply moved, Jesus reached out his hand and touched the man—something no one else would do. "I am willing," he said. "Be clean!"

⁴² Instantly the leprosy vanished, and the man was healed.

⁴³ Jesus sent him away at once with a stern warning:

⁴⁴ "Don't tell anyone about this. Just go, show yourself to the priest, and make the offering that Moses prescribed for your cleansing—that will be proof enough for them."

⁴⁵ But the man went out and couldn't stop talking about what had happened, spreading the story everywhere. As a result, Jesus could no longer walk openly into any town. He had to stay out in the lonely places—yet people kept coming to him from every direction.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Opening verse:** Mark wastes no time. There is no birth narrative, no genealogy—just a title card and action. I rendered "beginning" (ἀρχή) as "This is where... begins" to convey that Mark is launching a story already in motion.

2. **"The sky torn wide open" (v. 10):** The Greek σχιζομένους is violent—the same root word Mark uses when the temple curtain is "torn" at Jesus's death (15:38). This is not a gentle parting of clouds. I used "torn wide open" to capture that force.

3. **"Drove him out" (v. 12):** Mark says the Spirit ἐκβάλλει (literally "throws out") Jesus into the wilderness—the same verb used for casting out demons. The Spirit's action is forceful and startling. I preserved this energy with "drove."

4. **"You bring me great joy" (v. 11):** The Greek εὐδόκησα ("I am well pleased") is more than intellectual approval. In context, this is a father's declaration of deep delight in his son. I rendered the emotional weight rather than the formal formula.

5. **"Dropped their nets on the spot" (v. 18):** The Greek εὐθύς (immediately) appears relentlessly in this chapter—Mark's signature breathless pace. Rather than repeating "immediately" mechanically, I varied the English to maintain the sense of urgency: "on the spot," "at once," "straight to," "right away," "just then."

6. **"Spread like wildfire" (v. 28):** The Greek simply says his report went out "immediately everywhere." The idiom captures both the speed and the uncontrollable nature of the spread.

7. **"Touched the man—something no one else would do" (v. 41):** I added this contextual note because the original audience would have understood the shocking social and religious transgression of touching a leper. Modern readers may not feel the same weight without it.

8. **Variant at v. 41:** Sinaiticus reads "moved with compassion." Codex Bezae reads "moved with anger." If Mark originally wrote "anger," it may refer to Jesus being angry at the disease itself or at the social system that isolated lepers. Both readings are theologically rich. I have followed Sinaiticus but note that the Bezae reading has strong scholarly support.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
